sentences.text: I'm Fred Kort.
I was born in 1923 in Germany, in Leipzig.
Of course.
I remember my grandparents because they lived in Vienna.
That's where my mother grew up.

So each year, or periodically, since I was a little child, the whole family went to Vienna and subsequently to a resort near Vienna by the name of [NON-ENGLISH] for our summer vacations, especially in the earlier years of my life.
And the last time, I believe I visited my grandparents in Vienna-- that must have been in 1936.
That was the year of my bar mitzvah.
And I remember at that time, that last visit in particular, my grandmother-- because my grandfather already had passed away.
And it was a very interesting visit.
And it was just shortly before the Anschluss, before the Nazis entered Austria.
So it was quite beautiful still and relatively tranquil.
Tranquility existed.
And that's about as much as I remember.
In the earlier years, I do remember my grandfather, when he was still alive.
And he came to visit us periodically and we went to visit him periodically.
His name was Johann Stoegerer.
No.
I was too young.
I don't know anything about his family.
Now incidentally, the grandparents that I just mentioned-- they were on my mother's side.
I don't remember anything about the grandparents of my father's side.
I believe they may have been dead, even before my father married my mother.
Yes, we did.
I know a-- quite a bit about my forefathers, and especially the group of my ancestors that originally lived in Europe, anywhere from England, to Austria, to Poland, to Germany.
And that family tree, incidentally, was created by a research group somewhere here, in-- somewhere in Ohio.
And they put that whole family tree together the best they could put it together.
Well, no, nothing in particular, just going back to-- to the Kort family in the 15th century, and so on, and so forth.
There is a book that was published.
That's-- incidentally, that's a company in Ohio that does things like that, relating to rare names in America.
That's how that family tree came about.
And in fact, they even researched and they-- they came up with the coat of arms that our ancestors used, and I have it--
--in my study here.
He was not.
No, they all--
Yeah
Except for one grandmother.
Except for that one grandmother.

Oh, I remember, you know, I-- while I was born in Leipzig, when I was still very young-- and of course, as I pointed out before and will point out again, I had a brother and a sister-- that when we-- when we were all still very small children, we moved to a little village by the name [NON-ENGLISH], near Heidelberg in southern Germany.
And we went to school there.
But it was a very small Jewish community.
And my parents wanted me, especially-- because my brother and my sister, they're each two years older than I.
And the oldest was my sister.
And my brother was two years older than I.
And they already started to learn a trade in some other cities in Germany.
And my parents wanted me to be with a larger Jewish community, so they sent me to a town near the Swiss and French border by the name of Breisach.
There was a relatively large Jewish community.
The total population may have been 4,000 of-- of that little city, but there may have been as many as 500 Jewish people in that town.
And there were a lot of Jewish children, so they sent me there.
And that's where-- incidentally, where I was bar mitzvahed.
And my parents came.
No, not in particular.
I was so engrossed in being with a number of other Jewish young people there in that little town.
Because in-- in that village, where I lived before, there-- it was not a very pleasant surrounding, especially when Hitler came to power.
I sure did.
It was-- it was certainly tremendously different, because I had a chance to be involved with more Jewish families, which was not possible.
Before I left where?
Yeah.
Incidentally, my comments that I made to you before we went on camera, is that I was bar mitzvahed in 1936.
Actually, I was bar mitzvahed in 1938.
I left that little village and subsequently went to that small town, where the larger Jewish family-- families were.
That was in-- that-- that was in 1936.
And yes, by all means, I remember quite a bit of anti-Semitism.
In fact, I remember precisely the day-- the exact day-- which was January 1930, when Hitler was elected to power, became chancellor of the German Reich.
It was January 30, 1930.
Unless I am utterly wrong, but I believe that was the date.
Or maybe it was in 1933.
You might be correct.
I think it was 1933.
Let me correct myself.
It was January 30, 1933.
The reason I do remember, because Hitler's regime lasted for exactly 12 years.
So it was January 30th, 1933.
I want to make sure the date is correct.
Yes.
First, in that small village, of course, they always-- the young kids that knew you were Jewish-- there were just a handful of Jews there.
And perhaps between my brother and myself-- there may have been two, three other younger Jewish individuals.
And so we were sort of a rarity.
And so they-- they insulted us and called us names and reacted in an adverse way on our Jewishness.
That was in that little village.
And then later on, it intensified, even when I got to that other town, where there was a much larger Jewish community.
But as time progressed.
Yeah, I felt it very-- as time went on, anyways, I got there in 1936.
And it was fair, the situation.
Of course, I was very young.
I probably didn't understand what was going on around me.
But the anti-Semitism gradually increased.
And from school in the winter, the regular school, to a regular public school, and got insulted, and beaten up, and--
--you lousy Jew, and so on, and so forth.
And the teachers gradually became more and more anti-Semitic.
And you couldn't escape it.

Initially, yeah, that was a public school, while simultaneously, twice a week, we also went to the-- to the Jewish schools, to like-- we didn't call it cheder, but you would call a cheder-- to learn Hebrew and Jewish history.

At that time, there was another Jewish family that I was assigned to them through the-- through a Jewish organization that helped Jewish families to get their-- themselves settled and maybe-- and incidentally, we were not that well off.
We were a relatively [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not affluent family.
And at that time, incidentally, my father already left Germany, he-- because of the anti-Semitism.
And he went to Poland, as early as 1935, in fact.
But he visited us periodic, then left.
And he did some odd jobs there in Poland.
And he somehow sent-- was able to send us some money periodically, but not on a-- it was a real struggle for my mother and the three children.
She was basically alone at that time.
She must have missed us all, because my brother was another town.
He learned the metal industry, whatever, to learn about metals, and so on.
And my sister-- she worked for another Jewish family near Cologne.
So I-- but I was the youngest.
She would have missed me quite a bit.
And she came to visit me periodically.
But it was a good thing.
She was very happy that I was able to get myself settled there, in this town, where there-- where there was quite a sizable Jewish community.

As far as the Nazism is concerned, I sensed it probably as early-- even before the election in 1933, where there were-- there was quite a bit of oh, recruiting for the-- for the favored parties of the people, and demonstrations, and so on.
And you couldn't help but knowing.
And certainly, it was no secret that the Nazis were against the Jews.
So it-- so gradually, it just became part of you, that you had to be aware, and knowing what was going on around you.
Oh, that was a long time later.
And if I may go on chronologically--
So anyway, so I came to this town, Breisach, as I mentioned before.
And if I didn't, now I did.
I-- and it was quite nice for me.
But the-- the anti-Semitism gradually increased in school, and in the street, and wherever you were.

And I recall, in fact, that a certain number of Jewish families that had their children in public school, and that could afford it, took their sons or daughters out of-- from the public schools and sent them to a Jewish school on a daily basis.
They took the train from-- from Breisach to Freiburg, which is in the Black Forest-- a sizable city.
They went there every day, in the morning, and came back in the evening.
But my mother-- we couldn't afford it.
And the Jewish community-- at that particular time, they didn't want to spend the money.
So people-- unless the parents were able to fund that Jewish education, that other little city, for others it wasn't possible.
So in any case, so I lived in that little town.
And then in 1936, I was bar mitzvahed.
It was a very nice but small affair.
And my mother, and my brother, and my sister came.
And that was-- that event, two, three days, and then they went back and I stayed there.
And finally, in 1938, in April, I graduated from the public school.
And they-- and I was sent to a city by the name of Karlsruhe, which was the-- the capital of the province Baden.
And there, there was a Jewish electrical engineer, who was quite prominent in the city.
And he still had his business intact.
And I had a job to learn the elect-- I got a job with him as a trainee, as an apprentice, to learn electrical engineering.
So that was in April of 1938.
And there again, he-- his clientele by that time had shrunk basically to the Jewish businesses that needed that type of work.
Because of anti-Semitism, the non-Jews wouldn't subscribe to a Jewish engineer's in this town and the work that he was performing.
So-- and I worked there.
And I installed and I learned the trade.

And the next basic event-- main event, in my view, as I remember-- that took place was when I received a postal card from my mother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about-- that would have been maybe the first or second day of November of 1938.
And she writes in that card that she, my brother, my sister-- they were deported from Germany because of their stateless or Polish citizenship.
Even though we children were born in Germany there, unlike than other-- than in other countries-- the US, for example-- you are a citizen of the country what your father is a citizen of.
So my father-- he was born in Poland.
He came to Germany as a little child.
His parents nor he ever took out citizenship for Germany.

Among that group of Jews that were deported to Germany was a family by the name of Grynszpan, and a mother, a father, and I believe another couple of children, who at that time had a son who was a student-- whose son was a student in Paris.
The reason I mention this-- because later on, you will see what the connection was between it and the Kristallnacht.
So anyway, I received that postal card.
And obviously she had to-- my mother tells me they got kicked out and I should go back to our village and see what I could salvage and send back to them to Poland.
They didn't pick me up because I was a minor.
The only-- either a minor or because I was away from home and they probably couldn't find me, or whatever the situation may have been.
And at that time, I was only 14 years old-- 14 and 1/2, maybe 15.
And of course, it was very-- I-- there was very little I could do.
I didn't have the know-how to go back to this village and collect all that stuff.
It was very difficult.
I managed a little bit, but it was very, very difficult for me.

On the 8th of November of 1938, we heard that a vice ambassador, or whatever his rank was, of the German-- in the German embassy in Paris got assassinated by a Jew by the name of Herschel Grynszpan, who in fact was the son of that Grynszpan family that incidentally, later on, I met when I got to Poland, who were so upset, who most likely got similar message as I did, that his parents got kicked out from Germany and are without a home, and whatever, and experiencing tremendous hardship.
So he bought himself a gun.
He wanted to see the ambassador-- could get only as far as the vice ambassador by the name of vom Rath, and killed him.
I believe that was on the 8th of November of 1938.
And there was a lot of write-ups, all over the world, in-- in publications everywhere, about the incident.

On November 11th, two days later, my boss, the engineer Guggenheim-- we were working there install-- installing some electrical [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] systems, in a Jewish factory incidentally-- still, at that time.

And the gestapo, the civilian-- the secret police came and arrested us, picked us up right from the job, and took us to the central square of Karlsruhe, where the city hall and everything was located-- all the governmental buildings.
It was a-- you know, the capital of the province of Baden.
And there was a mob by the thousands.
And we had to walk through that group of quote, unquote, 'mad people.' And they beat us, and made us bloody, and it was awful.
And a bunch of other Jews-- and finally, they took us to a horse barn, somewhere where the police had their horses, and-- and again mistreated us, and so on-- insulted us.
And we had to face the walls.
And every few minutes, the-- over the loudspeaker, they had the loudspeaker saying, well, you don't have to cry.
It will be just a few more minutes.
We'll shoot you to death.
And of course-- and I was screaming.
You know, I-- I couldn't take it.
It was awful for me.
And-- and I got poked.
Don't scream, and then so on, and so forth.
But in any case, late that night-- and it must have been about 10:00 or 11:00-- they picked out all the minors, including me, and sent them home, sent them back.
My boss and, from that group, maybe 400 or 500 adult males were sent to Dachau.
That was the first association I heard of Dachau.
We heard of Dachau.
It was a penal camp for-- for-- for Jews and also for non-Jews that were against the Nazi regime.
And he was there.

And I went back to the-- I stayed with the-- in the boss's house, by the way, in the engineer, because it's-- it's a common thing when you go to work in those years, you stay with the boss, and he gives you room and board, and so on and so forth.
And of course, his wife, and his son, and his daughter-- they were all there.
They were terribly upset.
And finally, the order came, whoever pays enough money or has ways to leave Germany they would release from Dachau.
So about three, four weeks later, this engineer Guggenheim was released from Dachau and with his commitment and promise that he would leave Germany.
And he had some relatives in Bogota, Colombia.
So that's where they migrated.
Good.
